nodes.name: World Religions
nodes.description: Major world religions, founders, texts, and beliefs

items.prompt: Which religion, founded in 1st-century Palestine, is based on the teachings of Jesus of Nazareth and centers on the belief in his resurrection?
items.explanation:
Founded in 1st century CE in Roman Palestine; world's largest religion with ~2.4 billion adherents
Based on the life and teachings of Jesus of Nazareth, believed by followers to be the Son of God and Messiah
Sacred text is the Bible (Old Testament + New Testament); the Gospels of Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John narrate Jesus's life
Three major branches: Roman Catholicism (~1.3B), Protestantism (~900M), and Eastern Orthodoxy (~220M)
Key holy sites: Church of the Holy Sepulchre (Jerusalem), Vatican City (Rome), Church of the Nativity (Bethlehem)
The Nicene Creed (325 CE) established core doctrines including the Trinity — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit
Split into East and West in the Great Schism of 1054; Protestantism began with Martin Luther's 95 Theses in 1517
Christmas and Easter are its two principal holidays; the cross is its universal symbol
items.cardFront: Jesus of Nazareth, Bible, Trinity, ~2.4 billion adherents

items.prompt: Which religion, whose name means 'submission to God,' was founded in 7th-century Arabia by a merchant who received revelations in a cave near Mecca?
items.explanation:
Founded c. 610 CE in Mecca (modern Saudi Arabia); ~1.9 billion adherents, second-largest religion
Founded by the Prophet Muhammad (c. 570–632 CE), who received revelations from God (Allah) via the angel Gabriel
Sacred text is the Quran (114 chapters called suras); the Hadith records Muhammad's sayings and actions
Five Pillars: shahada (declaration of faith), salat (five daily prayers), zakat (almsgiving), sawm (Ramadan fasting), hajj (pilgrimage to Mecca)
Two major branches: Sunni (~85–90%) and Shia (~10–15%); split originated in a succession dispute after Muhammad's death in 632
Holy sites: Mecca (the Kaaba), Medina (Prophet's Mosque), and Jerusalem (Al-Aqsa Mosque / Dome of the Rock)
The Islamic Golden Age (8th–14th centuries) produced major advances in mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and philosophy
Fastest-growing major religion by birth rate; projected to nearly equal the number of adherents of the largest religion by 2050
items.cardFront: Prophet Muhammad, Quran, Five Pillars, ~1.9 billion adherents

items.prompt: Which religion, the world's oldest major faith still practiced, has no single founder and centers on concepts like dharma, karma, and moksha?
items.explanation:
Originated in the Indus Valley region; roots date to 2000–1500 BCE or earlier; ~1.2 billion adherents (third-largest)
No single founder — evolved from Vedic traditions, folk practices, and philosophical schools over millennia
Sacred texts include the Vedas (oldest, c. 1500 BCE), the Upanishads, the Bhagavad Gita, and the epics Mahabharata and Ramayana
Core concepts: dharma (duty/righteousness), karma (action and consequence), samsara (cycle of rebirth), moksha (liberation)
Major deities include Brahma (creator), Vishnu (preserver), and Shiva (destroyer) — the Trimurti
Holy sites include Varanasi (on the Ganges), Rishikesh, Haridwar, and the Char Dham pilgrimage circuit
Major traditions: Vaishnavism (Vishnu), Shaivism (Shiva), Shaktism (Devi/goddess worship)
The caste system (varna) and the festival of Diwali (Festival of Lights) are among its most widely known cultural features
items.cardFront: No single founder, Vedas, dharma/karma/moksha, ~1.2 billion adherents

items.prompt: Which religion was founded by a prince who renounced his wealth to seek enlightenment under a Bodhi tree in northeastern India around the 5th century BCE?
items.explanation:
Founded c. 5th century BCE in northeastern India by Siddhartha Gautama; ~500 million adherents
Siddhartha Gautama (c. 563–483 BCE), a prince of the Shakya clan, became the Buddha ('Awakened One') after meditating under the Bodhi tree at Bodh Gaya
Core teaching: the Four Noble Truths — life involves suffering (dukkha), suffering arises from craving, suffering can end, the path to its end is the Eightfold Path
Three major branches: Theravada (Southeast Asia), Mahayana (East Asia), and Vajrayana (Tibet, Mongolia)
Sacred texts vary by tradition: Pali Canon (Tipitaka) for Theravada; Mahayana sutras include the Lotus Sutra and Heart Sutra
Key holy sites: Bodh Gaya (enlightenment), Sarnath (first sermon), Lumbini (birthplace, in modern Nepal), Kushinagar (death)
The Dalai Lama is the spiritual leader of Tibetan Vajrayana tradition; the current (14th) Dalai Lama, Tenzin Gyatso, won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1989
Rejected the Hindu caste system and the authority of the Vedas; emphasizes the Middle Way between asceticism and indulgence
items.cardFront: Siddhartha Gautama, Four Noble Truths, Eightfold Path, ~500M adherents

items.prompt: Which religion traces its covenant with God to Abraham and Moses, follows 613 commandments in its sacred law, and worships in synagogues?
items.explanation:
One of the oldest monotheistic religions; origins c. 2000–1500 BCE with the patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; ~15 million adherents
Moses received the Torah (first five books: Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy) at Mount Sinai — the foundational narrative
The Tanakh (Hebrew Bible) comprises Torah, Nevi'im (Prophets), and Ketuvim (Writings); the Talmud is the central text of rabbinic law and commentary
613 mitzvot (commandments) govern daily life; the Ten Commandments are the most widely known subset
Three major branches: Orthodox, Conservative, and Reform; Hasidic movements are a subset of Orthodox
Holy sites: Western Wall (Kotel) and Temple Mount in Jerusalem; the First Temple was built by Solomon, the Second destroyed by Rome in 70 CE
Shabbat (Friday sunset to Saturday sunset) is the weekly day of rest; major holidays include Passover, Yom Kippur, and Rosh Hashanah
The Holocaust (1941–1945) killed approximately 6 million Jews — roughly one-third of the world's Jewish population
items.cardFront: Abraham and Moses, Torah, 613 commandments, ~15M adherents

items.prompt: Which monotheistic religion was founded in 15th-century Punjab by Guru Nanak and is organized around a succession of ten gurus?
items.explanation:
Founded c. 1499 CE in the Punjab region (modern India/Pakistan) by Guru Nanak (1469–1539); ~25–30 million adherents
Ten human gurus from Guru Nanak (1st) to Guru Gobind Singh (10th, 1666–1708), who declared the scripture as the eternal guru
Sacred text: the Guru Granth Sahib (also called Adi Granth), compiled by Guru Arjan in 1604; treated as a living guru and given a ceremonial resting place each night
The Golden Temple (Harmandir Sahib) in Amritsar, Punjab, is the holiest site; its communal kitchen (langar) feeds up to 100,000 people daily for free
The Khalsa, founded by Guru Gobind Singh in 1699, requires the Five Ks: kesh (uncut hair), kangha (comb), kara (steel bracelet), kachera (undergarment), kirpan (ceremonial dagger)
World's fifth-largest religion; ~90% of adherents live in India, mostly in Punjab state
Emphasizes equality, honest work, sharing with others, and meditation on God's name (Naam Japna)
Operation Blue Star (1984) — Indian Army's controversial assault on the Golden Temple — led to the assassination of Prime Minister Indira Gandhi by her Sikh bodyguards
items.cardFront: Guru Nanak, Guru Granth Sahib, Golden Temple, ~25–30M adherents

items.prompt: Which Chinese philosophical and religious tradition, attributed to a sage who reportedly wrote its foundational text before disappearing through a mountain pass, emphasizes living in harmony with 'the Way'?
items.explanation:
Originated in China, 4th–6th century BCE; attributed to Laozi (Lao Tzu), whose historical existence is debated
Foundational text: the Tao Te Ching (Daodejing), ~5,000 characters long — one of the most translated books in history after the Bible
The Tao ('the Way') is the fundamental, nameless force underlying all of reality; the opening line: 'The Tao that can be told is not the eternal Tao'
Yin and yang — complementary opposites — and wu wei (effortless action / non-interference) are central concepts
The Zhuangzi, attributed to the philosopher Zhuang Zhou, is the other major early text; famous for the 'butterfly dream' parable
Religious Taoism developed later with temples, priests, rituals, and an elaborate pantheon of deities and immortals
Influenced Chinese medicine, martial arts (tai chi, qigong), feng shui, and traditional Chinese landscape painting
Estimated 12 million formal adherents, though Taoist ideas permeate Chinese culture far more broadly
items.cardFront: Laozi, Tao Te Ching, yin and yang, wu wei

items.prompt: Which indigenous Japanese religion centers on the worship of kami — spirits inhabiting natural phenomena, ancestors, and sacred places?
items.explanation:
Indigenous religion of Japan; no single founder; roots in prehistoric Japanese animism and nature worship
The word means 'way of the gods' (shin = gods/spirits, to = way); coined to distinguish native beliefs from imported Buddhism
Sacred texts: the Kojiki ('Records of Ancient Matters,' 712 CE) and Nihon Shoki ('Chronicles of Japan,' 720 CE) contain the creation myths
Kami are spirits or gods inhabiting natural features, ancestors, and phenomena; Amaterasu (sun goddess) is the chief kami and mythical ancestor of the imperial family
The Grand Shrine at Ise (Ise Jingu), rebuilt every 20 years, is the holiest site; Japan has roughly 80,000 Shinto shrines
Torii gates (red/orange portal gates) mark the entrance to sacred space; visitors purify with water at temizu basins
State Shinto was the official ideology of imperial Japan (1868–1945); Emperor Hirohito renounced his divinity in 1946
Most Japanese practice elements of both Shinto and Buddhism simultaneously — weddings are often Shinto, funerals Buddhist
items.cardFront: Indigenous to Japan, kami worship, Kojiki, torii gates

items.prompt: Which ethical and philosophical system, originating from a 6th-century BCE Chinese teacher, emphasizes filial piety, ritual propriety, and the cultivation of virtue through the Five Relationships?
items.explanation:
Founded by Confucius (Kong Fuzi, 551–479 BCE) in the state of Lu during China's Spring and Autumn period
The Analects (Lunyu), compiled by his disciples, contain his core teachings on ethics, governance, and moral self-cultivation
Canonical texts: the Four Books (Analects, Mencius, Great Learning, Doctrine of the Mean) and Five Classics (Book of Odes, Book of Documents, Book of Changes, Book of Rites, Spring and Autumn Annals)
Five Key Relationships: ruler–subject, parent–child, husband–wife, elder–younger sibling, friend–friend; each carries reciprocal duties
Ren (benevolence/humaneness), li (ritual propriety), and xiao (filial piety) are the three core virtues
Became China's state philosophy under Emperor Wu of the Han Dynasty (r. 141–87 BCE); remained dominant for over 2,000 years
The imperial examination system, based on mastery of Confucian classics, selected Chinese bureaucrats from 605 CE until 1905
Debated whether it is a religion or philosophy — has no deity worship or afterlife doctrine, but includes ancestor veneration and ritual
items.cardFront: Confucius, Analects, filial piety, Five Relationships

items.prompt: Which ancient Persian religion, founded by a prophet whose name gives it its modern designation, teaches a cosmic struggle between Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu?
items.explanation:
Founded by the prophet Zoroaster (Zarathustra) in ancient Persia; dating disputed — estimates range from 1500 BCE to 600 BCE
Sacred text: the Avesta, whose oldest portion (the Gathas) are hymns attributed directly to Zoroaster
Central theology: cosmic dualism between Ahura Mazda (Wise Lord, good) and Angra Mainyu (Destructive Spirit, evil)
Was the state religion of three successive Persian empires: Achaemenid, Parthian, and Sasanian (c. 550 BCE–651 CE)
Fire is sacred as a symbol of Asha (truth/righteousness); fire temples maintain an eternal flame; practitioners sometimes called 'fire worshippers'
Parsis — Zoroastrians who fled to India after the Arab conquest of Persia (7th–10th centuries) — are the largest surviving community
Influenced Judaism, Christianity, and Islam with concepts of heaven/hell, final judgment, angels/demons, and a messiah figure
Today roughly 100,000–200,000 adherents remain worldwide, mostly in India (Parsis) and Iran; Freddie Mercury was born into a Parsi family
items.cardFront: Zoroaster, Avesta, Ahura Mazda vs. Angra Mainyu, ancient Persia

items.prompt: Which Indian religion, whose adherents practice radical nonviolence (ahimsa) to the extent of sweeping paths to avoid stepping on insects, was shaped by 24 spiritual teachers called tirthankaras?
items.explanation:
Ancient Indian religion; the last tirthankara, Mahavira (c. 599–527 BCE), is the key historical figure; ~4–5 million adherents, mostly in India
Twenty-four tirthankaras ('ford-makers') are the spiritual teachers; Mahavira was the 24th, Rishabhadeva the legendary 1st
Ahimsa (nonviolence) is the supreme principle — extends to all living beings; Jain monks may wear mouth coverings to avoid inhaling insects
Sacred texts: the Agamas, compiled from Mahavira's teachings; the Tattvartha Sutra is accepted by all Jain sects
Two major sects: Digambara ('sky-clad,' monks practice nudity) and Shvetambara ('white-clad,' monks wear white robes)
Three core vows (expanded to five for monks): ahimsa (nonviolence), satya (truth), asteya (non-stealing), brahmacharya (chastity), aparigraha (non-attachment)
Jains are disproportionately influential in Indian business and philanthropy despite small numbers; historically prominent in banking and trade
The Ranakpur and Dilwara temples in Rajasthan are among the most intricate carved-marble structures in the world

items.prompt: Which religion, founded in 19th-century Persia, teaches the unity of all religions and of all humanity, and has its world headquarters on Mount Carmel in Haifa, Israel?
items.explanation:
Founded in 1863 by Baha'u'llah (Mirza Husayn-Ali Nuri, 1817–1892) in Persia (modern Iran); ~5–8 million adherents in over 200 countries
Grew out of Babism, a movement founded by the Bab (Siyyid Ali Muhammad, 1819–1850), who declared himself a messianic forerunner
Central teaching: progressive revelation — God sends messengers (including Abraham, Moses, Jesus, Muhammad, the Bab, Baha'u'llah) suited to each age
Sacred texts: the Kitab-i-Aqdas ('Most Holy Book'), Kitab-i-Iqan ('Book of Certitude'), and the Hidden Words, all by Baha'u'llah
World headquarters: the Baha'i World Centre on Mount Carmel, Haifa, Israel; the Shrine of the Bab with its golden dome is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Baha'u'llah spent 40 years in exile and imprisonment (Baghdad, Constantinople, Adrianople, Acre); wrote prolifically throughout
Teaches the elimination of prejudice, equality of men and women, universal education, harmony of science and religion, and a world federal system
Heavily persecuted in Iran since the 1979 revolution; Baha'is are the largest non-Muslim religious minority in Iran
items.cardFront: Baha'u'llah, Kitab-i-Aqdas, unity of religions, Mount Carmel (Haifa)
